Details about the selected Funds:

(SCHD) Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF and (ADX) Adams Diversified Equity Fund:

Together, these two funds will take a major share of 40% of the portfolio, and will serve as the foundation of our portfolio. SCHD is an ETF, while ADX is a non-leveraged CEF (closed-end fund). SCHD provides income based on what it earns, while ADX (being a CEF) provides 'managed' income, as it is committed to providing roughly 8% of its average NAV on a yearly basis. Together, they will provide a roughly 6% yield. Please note that both funds use large-cap stocks from the S&P 500 and may have overlap in holdings.

SCHD is one of the most popular dividend ETFs. It is generally invested in large blue-chip dividend-paying stocks from the S&P 500 index. The fund offers wide diversification, exposure to large blue-chip dividend stocks, moderately high-income yield, very low fees, and an impressive record of dividend growth. SCHD currently pays a 3.77% yield (TTM yield of 3.67%, 30-day SEC yield being 3.83%). SCHD's dividend payout can vary every quarter based on its underlying holdings. The fund's fee structure is very low at 0.06%, which means only $6 is charged (annually) on a capital base of $10,000. The fund's official benchmark is the Dow Jones U.S. Dividend 100 Index. Also, SCHD has a solid record of growing its dividends, and the dividend growth has been 10.4% during the last 5 years.

The ADX fund also invests in large-cap blue-chip stocks from the S&P 500, both dividend-paying and non-dividend-paying. Distributions are paid mostly from investment income and realized capital gains on a 'managed' basis. The fund has a very long and successful history of paying consistent distributions. The fee structure is reasonable at 0.64% (in comparison to other CEFs of a similar nature).

The Top 10 holdings of SCHD are AbbVie (ABBV), Merck (MRK), Amgen (AMGN), Lockheed Martin (LMT), Cisco (CSCO), Verizon (VZ), Altria (MO), ConocoPhillips (COP), Chevron (CVX), and Home Depot (HD).

The Top 10 holdings of ADX are: Microsoft (MSFT), NVIDIA (NVDA), Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOGL), Meta (META), Broadcom (AVGO), JPMorgan Chase (JPM), Adams Natural Resources (PEO), and Netflix (NFLX).

(JEPQ) JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF:

While our first two funds covered the stocks from the S&P 500 and focused on providing income, our next pick is aimed at both income and higher growth. Retirees need growth, as their portfolios need to last 30 years or more. Inflation is a constant factor that is chipping away at your spending power. Without growth, most portfolios will not last 30 years.

JEPQ is offered by the JPMorgan group and invests primarily in Nasdaq-100 stocks. So, in that sense, it is concentrated in technology and high-growth stocks. Since, for usual blue-chip dividend-paying stocks, we already have SCHD and ADX, JEPQ will provide the exposure to high-growth stocks. At the same time, it provides a high level of income on a monthly basis that not only provides flexibility to withdraw for retirees' expenses, but part of it can be reinvested.

The fund writes covered calls on part of its underlying portfolio to generate high income while also providing some limited downside protection. However, nothing comes free; this also means that the upside of its underlying holdings will be somewhat limited, and growth performance will be subpar compared to the Nasdaq-100 index, especially in a bull market.

The fund uses a rather complex and unique approach of using ELNs (Equity-Linked-Notes) instead of writing actual calls. As per the fund, the ELNs that the fund invests in are derivative instruments that are designed to offer the economic benefit/loss of the covered calls written on the Nasdaq index. It writes monthly covered calls on the Nasdaq Index using weekly tranches with OTM (out-of-money) strikes, which allows it to capture a reasonable (but not all) of the upside while earning decent premiums to generate income. The 30-day SEC yield is 9.45%, while the 12-month rolling yield is 11.03%.

The top holdings in the ETF are large technology stocks, for example, NVIDIA (NVDA), Microsoft (MSFT), Apple (AAPL), Alphabet (GOOGL), Broadcom (AVGO), Amazon (AMZN), Meta (META), Tesla (TSLA), Netflix (NFLX), and JPMorgan US Govt MMkt (MGMXX). The top 10 holdings represent roughly 47% of the assets.

(HELO) JPMorgan Hedged Equity Laddered Overlay ETF

Or (JHQAX) JPMorgan Hedged Equity Fund:

This fund is offered by the JPMorgan group and not only provides exposure to U.S. large-cap stocks but also provides a hedging mechanism. Investors could either use the JPMorgan Hedged Equity Fund (JHQAX) or its similar ETF version, JPMorgan Hedged Equity Laddered Overlay ETF (HELO). JHQAX has nearly 12 years of history, while HELO is a pretty new ETF and has less than two years of history. Please note that there are some subtle differences between JHQAX and HELO, and their performance may not match exactly. These hedged equity funds aim to provide decent growth and protection against large market downturns. For example, in the year 2022, JHQAX lost only about 8% while S&P500 lost roughly 16%. But it has also underperformed the SP500 over the years, but nearly matches the performance of a 60:40 stock/Bond portfolio or any balanced index fund. This kind of fund helps investors to participate in equity market gains while mitigating risk in a declining market.

(PAAA) PGIM AAA CLO ETF

Or (JAAA) Janus Henderson AAA CLO ETF:

We are allocating 15% of the portfolio to this asset class, and the main purpose is to provide risk-adjusted high yield (higher than cash). We provide two choices above, with PAAA having slightly higher quality underlying assets than JAAA but with a lower yield. These funds primarily invest in high-quality AAA-rated CLOs (Collateralized Loan Obligations). These CLOs are senior corporate loans (floating-rate loans), but being AAA-rated, they are considered highly safe and have very low credit and default risk. In fact, there have been zero defaults (from these assets) during the last 30 years. Due to these reasons, the volatility is generally very low. These funds generally provide low correlation to traditional fixed income asset classes while providing lower volatility and downgrade risk.

Both PAAA and JAAA's portfolios are nearly 100% composed of high-quality AAA-rated CLO tranches (with PAAA having a slight edge). In other words, their portfolios hold the highest quality tranches of CLOs and are designed to deliver stable income to their investors.

We are allocating 15% of the portfolio to this fund, and the main purpose is to provide diversification to an alternative and high-quality asset with predictable income. Please note that the income is likely to fall as the Fed cuts interest rates. In addition, since this is a cash-like (not exactly though) investment, overall returns are likely to be much lower than stocks.

(SHY) iShares 1-3 Year Treasury Bond ETF

Or (SGOV) iShares 0-3 Month Treasury Bond ETF:

We are allocating a small portion of the portfolio to short-term Treasury funds. This will help in keeping the overall portfolio conservative and having lower volatility. This will also provide a retiree with flexibility in the form of emergency cash. Both of these ETFs are short-term Treasury bonds, while SGOV is very short-term (duration 1-3 months). As an alternative, one could also use laddered bank CDs with similar yields. We can use either fund, but a mix of both will be preferable. Currently, the yields for SGOV and SHY are reasonably attractive at 4.28% and 3.88%, respectively. However, yields are expected to fall gradually as the Fed will likely continue reducing the interest rates. The fund SGOV is almost as good as cash and will protect the overall portfolio from stock market drawdowns.

How to Deal with "Risk of Sequence of Returns"?

This is relevant for folks who may be retiring now or within the next few months to a year and plan to withdraw income to meet expenses.

To avoid the risk of sequence of returns (defined earlier), we recommend using a "rotational" strategy on at least on part of your portfolio. We will suggest one such strategy that not only produces income but is also likely to provide market-matching total returns. While it does that, it will provide the necessary protection from any deep corrections in the stock market. Here is the strategy:

We will invest in four primary securities plus one security as the hedging security. We will invest in the four primary securities on a continuous basis (each with 25% allocation), but would replace a security with the hedging security whenever it has performed worse (over the past few months) than the risk-free return provided by Cash (or 1-3 month Treasury). We check this on a monthly basis. This is explained in more detail a bit later.

This strategy is not a buy-and-hold type. Instead, it requires monthly trading, but mostly one or two trades a month. For this reason, it will be much more preferable to have this bucket inside a tax-deferred type of account.

The following are the four securities for regular use, plus one security as the hedging security.

Table-3: List of 5 securities:

Note: QLD is an ultra QQQ (Nasdaq-100), and it aims to provide 2x returns of QQQ after fees. We do not recommend QLD as a buy-and-hold investment, but this is inside a rotation portfolio, which means it will be held only when it is performing well. More conservative investors could replace it with QQQ.

The detailed steps to determine the monthly rotation:

Step-1: At the end of the month, calculate the average performance based on 3-month and 7-month periods for all four regular securities. The performance would mean total returns, including any dividends/distributions. Also, calculate the returns provided by risk-free Cash (or 1-3 month Treasury) over the same period.

Step-2: Compare the performance of each of the regular four securities (ADX, RQI, KYN, QLD) with the risk-free return provided by Cash. If it is worse, then replace that security with IEF (hedging security) for the next month.

Step-3: We will keep the investment from Step-2 for the entire next month.

Step-4: At the end of the following month, we will repeat the process (from Step-1 through 3).

Backtested Performance – Hedging/Rotational Bucket:

In addition to providing hedging and market-beating growth, this rotation strategy can also provide a significant amount of monthly income (our rough estimate at 6%). Three of the four securities selected provide high income (close to 8%), while the fourth one, QLD, provides almost no income. Also, due to rotation, at times, the hedging security with a lower yield may replace the regular security with a higher yield. So, on average, we expect the portfolio income to be roughly 6%.

Risks:

All investments carry some level of risk. Please consider the likely risks and your own risk tolerance before making any investments.

The portfolio mentioned in this article has roughly 7.5% cash equivalents and another 15% allocation to an 'AAA' rated CLO fund that provides higher income than cash but adds some risk. The rest of the roughly 75% portfolio is mostly equity but highly diversified. Some portion is hedged as well, but the majority is not. So, 75% of the portfolio will have similar or slightly less volatility than the broader market. However, this portfolio provides 6% plus income that can smooth the ride during market corrections.

Concluding Thoughts:

This article is not about whether $500,000 is enough to retire on. You could read our previous article on that topic (link provided earlier). This article discusses how to successfully invest $500,000 for retirement income and growth to last over 30 years or more of retirement. It assumes that no more than 5% to 6% of income is withdrawn annually.

To provide simplicity for passive investors, we have recommended a fund-based portfolio with only six funds. Out of these six funds, one is a cash-like security, while a second one provides higher income than cash but some added risk, and is still relatively safe. We have also included a fund that provides hedging, resulting in significantly reduced volatility and drawdowns, but also lower growth than the S&P 500. Overall, 65% of the portfolio is directed towards income and growth from equities.

As a bonus, for those who may be interested, we provide a rotation-based strategy to specifically deal with the risk of the sequence of returns in the initial years of retirement.
